clinicalStem: A 29-year-old man presents to his GP with a 4-day history of fever, severe headache, and back pain followed by a rash. He recently returned from West Africa. On examination, temperature is 38.8°C and there are pleomorphic lesions on the face, trunk, palms, and soles — all appearing at the same stage of development (deep-seated, umbilicated papules progressing to pustules). He also has prominent bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Monkeypox classically causes a prodrome of fever and marked lymphadenopathy (distinguishing it from chickenpox and smallpox) followed by a synchronous rash — all lesions at the same stage — that involves the palms and soles. Travel history from an endemic region (West Africa) is a key epidemiological clue. Chickenpox lesions appear in crops at different stages and spare the palms/soles. Smallpox is eradicated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — synchronous deep-seated rash with palmoplantar involvement, prodromal lymphadenopathy, and West African travel all point to monkeypox.
Varicella presents with lesions in multiple stages simultaneously (macules, papules, vesicles, crusts), typically spares palms/soles, and does not cause prominent lymphadenopathy.
Secondary syphilis can involve palms and soles but presents as a non-itchy macular/papular rash without umbilication or deep-seated pustules, and lacks the febrile prodrome with lymphadenopathy.
Disseminated HSV is seen in immunocompromised patients; lesions are vesicular, not deep-seated umbilicated pustules, and palmoplantar involvement is atypical.
Smallpox has been eradicated since 1980; the rash is identical in distribution but this diagnosis is not considered in current clinical practice outside bioterrorism scenarios.
clinicalPearl: Prominent lymphadenopathy is the single most useful clinical feature distinguishing monkeypox from both chickenpox and smallpox.
keyClue: Synchronous deep-seated umbilicated pustules on palms and soles with bilateral lymphadenopathy after travel to West Africa.
commonExamTrap: Mistaking monkeypox for chickenpox — chickenpox lesions are in different stages at the same time and spare palms/soles.
missedConcept: Monkeypox always produces lymphadenopathy, unlike smallpox or varicella; palmoplantar involvement is a key distinguishing feature.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old girl presents with a 2-day history of itchy blisters spreading from the trunk to the face and limbs. Her mother reports the rash started as red spots, some of which are now blisters while others have already crusted. Temperature is 37.9°C. The child has not been vaccinated. The GP suspects varicella but the school requires laboratory confirmation before the child can return. Which investigation is the MOST appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: PCR for varicella-zoster virus (VZV) from vesicle swab
Tzanck smear from the base of a blister
explanation: PCR of vesicular fluid or a swab from the base of a fresh blister is the gold-standard confirmatory test for VZV infection, offering high sensitivity and specificity. It can also distinguish VZV from herpes simplex virus. Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells but is not VZV-specific. Serology (IgM) can be falsely negative early and requires a second sample for seroconversion. Biopsy and CBC are non-specific.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — VZV PCR from vesicular fluid is the gold standard for confirming varicella.
Tzanck smear demonstrates multinucleated giant cells confirming a herpetic infection but cannot distinguish between VZV and HSV — not specific enough for formal confirmation.
Serum VZV IgM may be undetectable in the first few days of illness and requires a convalescent sample for seroconversion; PCR is faster and more reliable acutely.
Skin biopsy is invasive, slow, and not routinely used when non-invasive molecular testing is available.
FBC and CRP may show lymphocytosis or leukopenia but are non-specific and do not confirm the diagnosis of varicella.
clinicalPearl: VZV PCR from a fresh vesicle swab is the gold-standard test for confirming varicella; it is more sensitive and specific than Tzanck smear or serology.
keyClue: Polymorphic rash (macules, vesicles, crusts at the same time) on a non-vaccinated child — need laboratory confirmation.
commonExamTrap: Selecting Tzanck smear — it confirms herpetic infection but does NOT differentiate VZV from HSV.
missedConcept: Serology can miss acute VZV because IgM rises 4–7 days after onset; PCR gives a rapid, virus-specific result from vesicular material.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation presents with generalised itchy vesicular rash for 2 days. She has no history of chickenpox or vaccination. On examination, crops of vesicles in different stages are seen on the trunk, face, and scalp. Temperature is 38.3°C. She is concerned about her baby. Serology sent today shows VZV IgG negative and IgM positive. What does this serological result MOST likely indicate?
options: Primary varicella infection in a non-immune pregnant woman
Past immunity with no current infection
False positive due to cross-reactivity with herpes simplex IgM
explanation: IgG negative combined with IgM positive in the context of a compatible clinical presentation (classic chickenpox rash) in a non-immune, non-vaccinated individual confirms primary varicella infection. In pregnancy, this is particularly dangerous due to risk of congenital varicella syndrome before 20 weeks and severe maternal pneumonitis. Reactivation (zoster) would be dermatomal, and a previously immune person would already have IgG.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — IgG negative (no prior immunity) + IgM positive = acute primary VZV infection, which is high-risk in the first and second trimester of pregnancy.
Herpes zoster is reactivation in a previously immune person (IgG would already be positive); the rash is dermatomal, not generalised, and IgM is not typically used to diagnose zoster.
Past immunity is excluded by the negative IgG result — a previously immune person would have detectable IgG.
Vaccine-induced immunity produces IgG positivity; this patient has no vaccination history and is IgG negative.
Cross-reactivity with HSV IgM is possible but clinically unlikely when the rash is classic for chickenpox; clinical context must always be integrated with serology.
clinicalPearl: In pregnancy, primary varicella (IgG negative, IgM positive) before 20 weeks carries a 1–2% risk of congenital varicella syndrome; antiviral therapy with aciclovir and varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG) should be initiated urgently.
keyClue: VZV IgG negative + IgM positive in a non-vaccinated, non-immune pregnant woman with classic chickenpox rash.
commonExamTrap: Assuming IgG positivity would be present in any current infection — IgG may not appear until 1–2 weeks after onset; early acute infection is defined by IgM alone.
missedConcept: The combination of IgG negativity and IgM positivity distinguishes primary infection from prior immunity or reactivation.
redFlagReason: Primary varicella in pregnancy risks congenital varicella syndrome and severe maternal pneumonitis; urgent specialist referral and antiviral treatment are required.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents to a primary health centre after being bitten on the hand by a stray dog in rural Thailand 4 hours ago. The wound is a deep puncture. He has never received rabies pre-exposure vaccination. The dog was not captured. He washed the wound with soap and water immediately after the bite. What is the MOST appropriate next step in management?
options: Administer rabies post-exposure prophylaxis (rabies vaccine + rabies immunoglobulin) immediately
Observe the patient for 14 days and vaccinate only if symptoms develop
Administer rabies vaccine alone without immunoglobulin as the wound was already washed
Prescribe oral amoxicillin and reassure the patient
Send a serum sample for rabies antibody testing before deciding on prophylaxis
explanation: A deep puncture wound from a stray dog in a rabies-endemic country in an unvaccinated person constitutes WHO Category III exposure — the highest risk. Immediate post-exposure prophylaxis with both rabies vaccine AND rabies immunoglobulin (RIG) is indicated. Wound washing is important but does not eliminate the need for full PEP. RIG provides immediate passive immunity while the vaccine elicits active immunity. Delay or partial treatment is unacceptable given that clinical rabies is invariably fatal.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — WHO Category III exposure in an unvaccinated person requires both rabies vaccine and rabies immunoglobulin without delay.
Rabies has a near-100% fatality rate once symptomatic; waiting for symptoms before treating is never appropriate — prophylaxis must be given pre-symptomatically.
Vaccine alone (without RIG) is reserved for previously vaccinated individuals; an unvaccinated person needs RIG for immediate passive immunity alongside the vaccine series.
Antibiotics address wound infection from bacteria; they have no role in rabies prevention. Reassurance alone is dangerous given the near-certain fatality of clinical rabies.
Serology is irrelevant in the acute post-exposure setting; the decision to treat is based on epidemiological risk and wound category, not antibody levels.
clinicalPearl: WHO Category III rabies exposure (deep wound, unvaccinated patient, high-risk animal in endemic region) mandates immediate administration of both rabies vaccine AND rabies immunoglobulin — never delay for serology or observation.
keyClue: Deep puncture from stray dog in Thailand (endemic), unvaccinated patient — WHO Category III exposure.
commonExamTrap: Giving vaccine alone without RIG — unvaccinated patients need passive immunoglobulin for immediate protection while the vaccine series takes effect.
missedConcept: Rabies immunoglobulin provides bridging passive immunity; it must be given with the first vaccine dose, infiltrated into the wound, and cannot be given after day 7 of the vaccine series.
redFlagReason: Rabies is essentially 100% fatal once symptomatic; failure to give timely post-exposure prophylaxis in a high-risk exposure results in a preventable death.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old farmer from a rural area of Myanmar presents with a 5-day history of fever, agitation, and bizarre behaviour. His family reports he was bitten by a bat 6 weeks ago but did not seek treatment. On examination, he is extremely agitated, unable to swallow water (he grimaces and has pharyngeal spasms when offered a drink), and has profuse salivation. He is oriented but terrified. There is no neck stiffness. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: The triad of hydrophobia (pharyngeal spasms on attempted swallowing), aerophobia, hypersalivation, and agitation in a patient with a prior bat bite after the classic incubation period (weeks to months) is pathognomonic of furious (encephalitic) rabies. Bacterial meningitis features neck stiffness. Tetanus causes trismus and opisthotonus. HSV encephalitis lacks the hydrophobia/bite history. Organophosphate toxicity features cholinergic excess (miosis, bradycardia) not pharyngeal spasms.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — hydrophobia, aerophobia, hypersalivation, and agitation after a documented animal bite = furious rabies until proven otherwise.
Bacterial meningitis causes neck stiffness (meningism), photophobia, and petechiae; hydrophobia and hypersalivation are not features.
Tetanus presents with trismus (lockjaw), risus sardonicus, and opisthotonus; it does not cause hydrophobia or hypersalivation, and there is no preceding animal bite.
HSV encephalitis causes temporal lobe features (personality change, aphasia, seizures) but not hydrophobia or pharyngeal spasms in the context of an animal bite.
Organophosphate poisoning produces SLUDGE syndrome (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI cramps, Emesis) with miosis and bradycardia — not hydrophobia or bat-bite history.
clinicalPearl: Hydrophobia (pharyngeal spasms on attempted drinking) + prior animal bite = rabies until proven otherwise; once symptomatic, rabies encephalitis is universally fatal.
keyClue: Hydrophobia (pharyngeal spasms) and hypersalivation after a bat bite 6 weeks prior.
commonExamTrap: Confusing hypersalivation with organophosphate poisoning — organophosphate lacks hydrophobia and the animal-bite history.
missedConcept: The incubation period for rabies is typically 1–3 months; a bite weeks before symptom onset is still clinically relevant.
redFlagReason: Symptomatic rabies encephalitis is invariably fatal; identification triggers isolation, palliative care, and public health notification.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old student is admitted with progressive agitation, hydrophobia, and pharyngeal spasms 8 weeks after a dog bite abroad. Rabies is strongly suspected. The attending physician wants to confirm the diagnosis ante-mortem. Which combination of investigations is MOST appropriate?
options: RT-PCR on saliva + direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) test on nuchal skin biopsy
CT brain followed by lumbar puncture for CSF viral culture
Skin swab for viral culture from a fresh vesicular lesion
explanation: Ante-mortem diagnosis of rabies is best achieved by RT-PCR on saliva (high viral load in salivary glands) combined with DFA or PCR on a nuchal skin biopsy (targets the nerve fibres around hair follicles). These tests have the highest sensitivity and specificity for ante-mortem confirmation. Serum IgG may be absent early or in immunocompromised patients. CSF culture is insensitive. MRI findings are non-specific. Rabies does not produce vesicular skin lesions.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — RT-PCR on saliva and DFA/PCR on nuchal skin biopsy are the recommended ante-mortem tests for rabies with highest diagnostic yield.
CSF in rabies is usually normal or shows mild pleocytosis; viral culture from CSF is insensitive, and LP carries risk in an encephalitic patient.
Serum IgG reflects past vaccination or late-stage infection; it is absent early in disease and unreliable as a sole diagnostic test, especially in unvaccinated patients where seroconversion is late.
Brain MRI may show signal changes in the brainstem, thalamus, and hippocampus, but findings are non-specific and do not confirm rabies.
Rabies virus does not cause skin vesicles; this test has no role in rabies diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Ante-mortem rabies diagnosis uses RT-PCR on saliva and DFA/PCR on nuchal skin biopsy targeting nerve fibres around hair follicles — the most sensitive combination available.
keyClue: Confirmed hydrophobia and animal bite history requiring ante-mortem laboratory confirmation.
commonExamTrap: Relying solely on serum antibody titres — rabies antibodies may be undetectable or minimal in unvaccinated symptomatic patients.
missedConcept: Saliva carries the highest viral load ante-mortem; nuchal skin biopsy detects virus in cutaneous nerve endings around hair follicles.

subtopic: Diagnosis of gonorrhoea in primary care
clinicalStem: A 23-year-old man presents to his GP with a 5-day history of thick, yellow-green urethral discharge and dysuria. He has had a new sexual partner in the past 3 weeks. On examination, frank purulent discharge is visible at the urethral meatus. Urine dipstick shows leucocytes but no nitrites. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: Profuse, thick, purulent yellow-green urethral discharge developing within a short incubation after a new sexual contact is characteristic of gonococcal urethritis (Neisseria gonorrhoeae). The incubation period is typically 2–7 days. Chlamydia typically causes a mucopurulent or watery discharge that is less profuse. Non-specific urethritis (NSU) caused by Mycoplasma is even more subtle. Bacterial vaginosis is a diagnosis specific to the vaginal ecosystem and does not apply to male urethritis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — copious purulent urethral discharge with short incubation after new partner is the classic presentation of gonococcal urethritis.
Chlamydia urethritis typically produces a scant, watery or mucopurulent discharge, or is entirely asymptomatic — not the florid purulent discharge seen here.
Bacterial vaginosis is a condition of the female vaginal flora; it does not cause urethral discharge in men.
Trichomonas vaginalis can cause urethritis in men but more commonly presents in women; it is less likely than gonorrhoea given the profuse purulent discharge.
Non-specific urethritis (Mycoplasma genitalium, Ureaplasma) tends to cause mild, watery discharge and is not associated with the volume or purulence described here.
clinicalPearl: Profuse purulent urethral discharge within 2–7 days of sexual exposure = gonorrhoea until proven otherwise; always co-test for chlamydia as co-infection is common.
keyClue: Thick yellow-green purulent urethral discharge, short incubation (3 weeks), new sexual partner.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing chlamydia — chlamydia causes minimal or no discharge and a much more indolent course than the florid purulence described here.
missedConcept: Gonorrhoea has a short incubation (2–7 days) and characteristically produces frank purulence; chlamydia is typically silent or causes only mild discharge.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the health centre with a 3-day history of runny nose, mild sore throat, sneezing, and low-grade fever of 37.5°C. There is no facial pain, no productive cough, and no neck stiffness. On examination, the throat is mildly erythematous without exudate, and the tympanic membranes are normal. Cervical lymph nodes are not enlarged. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Viral upper respiratory tract infection (common cold)
explanation: The triad of nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, sneezing, mild sore throat, and low-grade fever with no exudate, no significant lymphadenopathy, and a 3-day duration is classic for a viral URTI (common cold, typically rhinovirus). Streptococcal pharyngitis features tonsillar exudate, high fever, and anterior cervical lymphadenopathy (Centor criteria). Sinusitis requires facial pain and purulent nasal discharge beyond 7–10 days. Mononucleosis causes significant lymphadenopathy and constitutional illness. Influenza typically causes abrupt-onset high fever and myalgia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — mild symptoms, no exudate, no significant lymphadenopathy, and a short duration with low-grade fever fit viral URTI (common cold) best.
Streptococcal pharyngitis is characterised by high fever (>38.5°C), tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and absence of cough (Centor/McIsaac criteria) — not present here.

Acute bacterial sinusitis presents with facial pain/pressure, nasal congestion, and purulent nasal discharge lasting more than 7–10 days or worsening after initial improvement — not a 3-day mild illness.
Infectious mononucleosis causes prominent posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, significant fatigue, splenomegaly, and often a florid exudative pharyngitis — much more severe than described.
Influenza typically presents with abrupt onset of high fever (>38.5°C), severe myalgia, headache, and rigors — the sudden systemic severity is absent here.
clinicalPearl: Viral URTI (common cold) is characterised by rhinorrhoea, mild sore throat, low-grade fever, and sneezing without significant exudate or lymphadenopathy; antibiotics are not indicated.
keyClue: Mild symptoms with no exudate, no lymphadenopathy, low-grade fever, and rhinorrhoea — classic viral URTI pattern.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing antibiotics for a viral cold — the absence of exudate, high fever, and lymphadenopathy argues strongly against bacterial infection.
missedConcept: Distinguishing viral from bacterial URTI is critical to avoid antibiotic overuse; clinical scoring tools like Centor criteria help stratify streptococcal pharyngitis risk.

subtopic: Best investigation for chlamydia in an asymptomatic woman
clinicalStem: A 19-year-old woman attends a family planning clinic for oral contraception. She is sexually active with a new partner and has no urinary or vaginal symptoms. She asks about STI screening. Physical examination is unremarkable. Which investigation is the MOST appropriate to screen for Chlamydia trachomatis in this patient?
options: Nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) on a self-collected vulvovaginal swab
Cervical culture for Chlamydia on speculum examination
explanation: NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) on a self-collected vulvovaginal swab is the recommended first-line investigation for Chlamydia trachomatis screening. It has >95% sensitivity and is acceptable to patients as it avoids the need for speculum examination. First-catch urine is an acceptable alternative for NAAT. Culture is less sensitive and not preferred. Serology cannot distinguish current from past infection. Gram stain detects gonorrhoea (intracellular Gram-negative diplococci) but not chlamydia, and urine microscopy is non-specific.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — NAAT on a self-collected vulvovaginal swab is the gold standard for chlamydia screening with highest sensitivity and patient acceptability.
Chlamydia culture requires specialised cell culture media, is less sensitive than NAAT, is technically demanding, and is not the preferred diagnostic modality.
Chlamydia IgG serology reflects past exposure or chronic infection and cannot distinguish active current infection from previous resolved infection — not suitable for acute screening.
Urine microscopy may show leucocytes in urethritis but is entirely non-specific and does not identify the organism.
Gram stain identifies Gram-negative intracellular diplococci (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) but cannot detect Chlamydia, which is an obligate intracellular organism not visible on routine Gram stain.
clinicalPearl: NAAT is the gold-standard test for chlamydia; self-collected vulvovaginal swabs are as sensitive as clinician-collected swabs and improve patient acceptability and uptake.
keyClue: Asymptomatic sexually active young woman with a new partner requesting STI screening — needs non-invasive, high-sensitivity chlamydia test.
commonExamTrap: Choosing Gram stain for chlamydia — Gram stain only detects gonorrhoea, not chlamydia, because Chlamydia is an obligate intracellular organism.
missedConcept: Chlamydia is the most common bacterial STI and is often asymptomatic; NAAT is the only acceptable screening tool in current guidelines.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old unvaccinated boy is brought to the clinic with a 4-day history of high fever, cough, runny nose, and red eyes. His mother says the fever spiked to 39.5°C. Today she noticed a red blotchy rash starting on his face and spreading to his trunk and limbs. On examination, he has bilateral conjunctival injection and small white spots on the buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: The classic presentation of measles includes the 3 Cs (Cough, Coryza, Conjunctivitis), high fever, Koplik spots (white spots on the buccal mucosa — pathognomonic), followed by a maculopapular rash starting on the face and spreading centrifugally. The child is unvaccinated, making measles even more likely. Rubella has a milder prodrome, no Koplik spots, and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Roseola causes rash as the fever breaks. Scarlet fever has a sandpaper rash. Kawasaki disease features mucosal changes without Koplik spots.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — high fever, 3 Cs (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis), Koplik spots, and centrifugal maculopapular rash = classic measles.
Rubella presents with a milder illness, a fine maculopapular rash that clears in 3 days, posterior cervical/occipital lymphadenopathy, and no Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa.
Roseola infantum (HHV-6) causes high fever for 3–5 days in infants; the rash appears as the fever defervesces ('fever then rash'); it does not cause Koplik spots or the 3 Cs.
Scarlet fever produces a fine, sandpaper-like erythematous rash with circumoral pallor and strawberry tongue; it follows streptococcal pharyngitis and does not cause Koplik spots or conjunctivitis.
Kawasaki disease causes fever, conjunctivitis, and mucosal changes (cracked lips, strawberry tongue) but does not produce Koplik spots, the 3 Cs, or the classic cephalocaudal rash spread.
clinicalPearl: Koplik spots (white spots on buccal mucosa) are pathognomonic of measles and appear before the rash — their presence clinches the diagnosis even before laboratory confirmation.
keyClue: Koplik spots on buccal mucosa + cough + coryza + conjunctivitis + fever preceding cephalocaudal rash in an unvaccinated child.
commonExamTrap: Confusing measles with rubella — rubella is much milder, lacks Koplik spots, and has prominent posterior cervical lymphadenopathy.
missedConcept: Koplik spots are the pathognomonic enanthem of measles and appear 1–2 days before the maculopapular exanthem, making them the earliest clinical differentiator.

clinicalStem: A neonate is born at 38 weeks to a 24-year-old mother who had a maculopapular rash with posterior cervical lymphadenopathy at 10 weeks of pregnancy and did not receive antenatal care. The baby has microcephaly, bilateral cataracts, and a loud pansystolic murmur. Blood count shows thrombocytopenia, and skin examination reveals scattered petechiae giving a 'blueberry muffin' appearance. What is the MOST likely underlying diagnosis?
explanation: The classic triad of congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) is: cataracts (or glaucoma), congenital heart disease (classically patent ductus arteriosus or pulmonary artery stenosis), and sensorineural deafness. The 'blueberry muffin' rash from dermal erythropoiesis, thrombocytopenic purpura, and microcephaly are additional features. The maternal history of rash with posterior cervical lymphadenopathy in the first trimester is consistent with primary rubella — the highest risk period for CRS (>80% if infection is before 12 weeks).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — CRS triad (cataracts, heart defect, deafness), blueberry muffin skin, and first-trimester maternal rubella-compatible illness confirm congenital rubella syndrome.

Congenital CMV is the most common cause of non-hereditary sensorineural hearing loss and causes periventricular calcifications; it can cause blueberry muffin rash and microcephaly but does NOT cause cataracts or congenital heart disease as classic features.

Congenital toxoplasmosis causes the classic triad of hydrocephalus, chorioretinitis, and intracranial calcifications (periventricular) — cataracts and congenital heart disease are not typical features.
Congenital syphilis presents with snuffles (purulent rhinitis), periostitis, hepatosplenomegaly, and Hutchinson teeth — cataracts and structural heart disease are not characteristic.
Neonatal herpes simplex presents with skin vesicles, encephalitis, or disseminated infection in the neonatal period; it is not typically associated with cataracts or structural heart defects.
clinicalPearl: The classic CRS triad is cataracts + congenital heart disease (PDA/pulmonary stenosis) + sensorineural deafness; first-trimester infection carries >80% risk of fetal damage.
keyClue: Bilateral cataracts + congenital heart disease + blueberry muffin rash + microcephaly + maternal first-trimester rash with posterior cervical lymphadenopathy.
commonExamTrap: Confusing CRS with congenital CMV — CMV does NOT cause cataracts or structural heart disease; periventricular calcifications point toward CMV or toxoplasmosis.
missedConcept: The blueberry muffin appearance in CRS is caused by dermal haematopoiesis (extramedullary red blood cell production) secondary to thrombocytopenia, not purpura alone.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old girl presents with a 5-day history of fever, cough, coryza, and a maculopapular rash that began on the face and spread to the trunk. She received only one dose of MMR vaccine. The rash is now fading. Her GP suspects measles and needs to confirm the diagnosis for public health notification. Which investigation is the MOST appropriate at this stage?
options: Throat swab for measles virus culture
Serum measles-specific IgG at acute presentation only
Full blood count for lymphocyte count
explanation: Serum measles-specific IgM is the recommended serological test for confirming acute measles and is detectable from day 3–4 of the rash. It is the most practical confirmatory test in primary care and is required for public health notification. At day 5 of rash, IgM should be reliably positive. PCR on nasopharyngeal specimens can also confirm measles and is especially useful in vaccinated individuals where serology may be atypical, but IgM serology is the standard first-line approach. Culture is slow and not practical.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — measles IgM is detectable by day 3–4 of the rash and is the standard serological test for acute case confirmation for public health reporting.
Measles virus culture is technically demanding, requires specialised BSL-2/3 facilities, takes weeks, and is not used for routine clinical diagnosis.

Serum IgG alone at one time point cannot differentiate acute infection from prior vaccination or past infection; a 4-fold rise in IgG between paired acute and convalescent sera would be needed — impractical for timely notification.

RT-PCR on nasopharyngeal specimens can confirm measles by detecting viral RNA and is particularly useful in vaccinated individuals, but in unvaccinated patients with a typical presentation, IgM serology is the primary recommended confirmatory test.
A full blood count may show leucopenia and lymphopenia consistent with measles but is non-specific and cannot confirm the diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Measles IgM is detectable from day 3–4 of rash onset and is the standard confirmatory test; all confirmed cases must be notified to public health authorities.
keyClue: Day 5 of maculopapular rash with cough, coryza, and fever — IgM is now reliably detectable.
commonExamTrap: Using IgG alone as confirmation — a single IgG titre cannot confirm acute measles; IgM or a 4-fold rise in paired IgG is required.
missedConcept: Measles is a notifiable disease; laboratory confirmation with IgM serology is required for public health reporting even when the clinical diagnosis is clear.
